items.subtopic: Early Diagnosis and Management of MTC
items.question: A 43-year-old woman with a right thyroid nodule has a serum calcitonin concentration of 45 pg/mL. What is the best next step in her management?
options: Wait and see; perform clinical and biochemical examination every 6 months
Total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection
Repeated FNA for immunocytochemistry for calcitonin and measurement of calcitonin in the needle washout
items.explanation: Given that the serum calcitonin concentration is greater than 20 pg/mL but less than 100 pg/mL, it serves as an alert. The patient should undergo a repeated fine-needle aspiration (FNA) for immunocytochemistry for calcitonin and measurement of calcitonin in the needle washout to further investigate the thyroid nodule.
items.reference: MAIN CONCLUSIONS: 'Elevated basal serum calcitonin, especially a medium-low level (ie, <100 pg/mL (<29.2 pmol/L)), should not be immediately considered to be diagnostic of MTC. These medium-low levels can be further investigated by performing a stimulation test with the intravenous administration of calcium to confirm that the calcitonin is really secreted by MTC: a significant increase in serum calcitonin (usually 3 to 4 times the basal level) is observed in patients with MTC, but not in those with elevated basal serum calcitonin derived from other sources or artifacts.'
